speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Helena Cartwright Carlson was recorded at her home near Troy on July 6, 1975. The interviewer was Karen Purtee.

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: I thought it would be nice if we started back where you said you were living before you came here and why your father decided to come here and then about your train ride and all that was part of that.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Okay, you mean clear back to Minnesota? Sure, why not?

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: Well, of course I can't remember much about that when I was born in Minnesota and my parents moved on. In fact, I guess my father lived in South Dakota and so he came, he was courting my mother in Minnesota and came over there.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: And then when they got married they moved to, I don't know how come I was born in Minnesota if they were living in South Dakota?

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: That's a good question. That's just, yeah, I can't remember because I was too young. Anyway, we were in South Dakota about the first instance of anything that I can remember and that was the time I swallowed the barley straw. We were on our way to church and it was in the winter in the bobbist land and what I had barley straw in my throat or in my mouth for but anyway, got ahold and it went to crawl down and crawled and crawled and mother said, don't swallow, don't swallow and they got the team turned around and headed for home and you know what I did, I just swallowed and swallowed as hard and fast as I could and finally I said, it's down, it's gone, you know, but it was too late and so we kept on going. And then I remember getting a pair of shoes.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: I remember that, it must have been there and I was so tiny I can't remember but except the clerk said, how did they feel?

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: And I was a little bit of a kid sitting there, really little. Oh, fine. And father missed me, went outside, he missed me, came back, I'd gone back in because I wanted another pair of shoes. Those didn't fit apparently. But then we came out west and settled in Orchard Track south of Spokane. That orchard business wasn't suitable to my dad, he didn't want to raise fruit. So they bought a place up north of Spokane at Valley Washington where he wanted to raise cows, dairy. He was interested in dairy. So they had a large herd of dairy cattle and amongst them were some wild ones, wild cows, and the only one that could handle them was my dad. So when he'd have to be gone, when he'd have to get someone else, they had them all timbers to hold them so that when anyone would milk them they'd have to sit on the outside and reach underneath these timbers because they kicked like mules, fought like fiends. You just can't imagine anything that was as wild as those cows were. But they weren't all wild. But anyway, we were there, I think it was four years and that's where I started school. Well, when I was south of Spokane I got old enough I guess, but my mother had taught me so I could read. I'd learned a few lessons in obedience too there because that's when I had the little doll buggy. My sister had a doll buggy and we were wheeling it around the field and we wanted to go over to the neighbors and she said, no we couldn't. So we wheeled around the fields and wheeled around the fields until we got real close. We got to the fence that was separating us from the neighbors and we decided as long as we got that close we'd just slide under the fence and go in just long enough to say hello and leave, which we did. But we found out that mother said no, she didn't know when we got back we got a good paddling. So we didn't try that anymore. But anyhow, then after we'd moved to Valley I went to school there and it took me six weeks to finish the first grade because my mother had done a real good job of teaching me. So I entered the first grade. I suppose maybe we got there at the end of the term or something. Anyway, I was very shy and very fearful there. We had a teacher after, I suppose I would have been in the second or third grade and we had a little teacher, her name was Tilly Colser by the way. And she was one of these, I think she made a resolution that before she finished that term of school she was going to give everybody in school a good little lickin'. And I think she did. She missed me somewhere rather, but I got threatened if I ever did another stunt like I had done, which was just, we were practicing a drill for a program. And I stopped too soon in the drill. I was supposed to go and clear around the group. Instead I stopped at the end where I was supposed to wind up and she said, if you ever do that again you're going to get a whipping. Well I didn't do it again.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: What was the drill?

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: Yeah, a drill. There were, oh I suppose there would have been about ten, twelve kids and they lined up and I was one of the little ones at one end. There was another girl, a little one at the other end. And they divided seeing they go like this and they make fancy movements. Oh it's a walking drill. Yes, a walking drill. And I was at this one end and we were supposed to go and meet like this in some way or other and I was supposed to wind, go clear across and wind up at this end and when I got to that end I stopped. And that spoiled the looks of the drill. I was supposed to stop yet. I was supposed to go and clear around and come back.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Did you do that to music or did you?

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: Yes, to music. To music. Mm -hmm. They put on drills. That was one of the things they used to do in school. A lot of those, that was one of their fancy things they did was put on fancy drills. They dressed them and fixed them up, felt pretty and they must have been beautiful. Sure they were. For the parents to come see. Yeah and then they'd have a program for the parents to come. So that, I just remember that one and my little younger sister, I remember she got a whopping. She was daydreaming, sitting looking out of the window, thinking of things outside I guess. She got threatened once I believe. She was threatened once. The next time she got it. I felt worse than she did I guess. Anyway then after we, my dad said that if he was going to, if he could find a place. No he was going to, he decided that he'd sell out. He didn't like it there. So after we'd been there for four years why he moved to, must have been moved to Spokane. While they transitioned from one place to another. He found another place. So we lived in Hillard and he worked in the machine shops but he wasn't cut out for a machine shop man. We had more trouble in that winter that we were there. I'm sure than they've ever had and than they had ever had. He was sick and got hurt. Everybody was sick and had terrible things happen to him. So we, my dad then started out and he said if he could find a place where they could raise grain and hay. He was going to get it and go back into the dairying. I don't remember how it happened. Anyway, how he happened here about Troy. Anyway he came to Troy and they bought, I believe it's an 80 acre farm out here. He came back and we were all so thoroughly delighted because we were going to move back in the farm. None of us liked the city life. We did not like it. So we were happy as larks to get the chance to move back. While we were in here, we had acquired a little kitten which we named Tommy and a little brown pullet chicken had come there which turned into a pullet. She was just a chicken when we got her. How we acquired her I don't know. Anyway, she had grown up to a nice pullet and we couldn't bear to leave and not take our kitten and our pullet with us. So that was our livestock. They fixed us each a box. So my sister carried one and I carried the other and we boarded the train carrying our livestock with us. We came on the train to Troy and there were a lot of men on the train and they were talking a language we never had heard. We couldn't understand what they were talking about and a lot of them got off at Troy. Afterward we heard that they were lumberjacks. They had been to Spokane for a weekend or something I guess which was the big highlight of the lumberjacks that those times they would work and earn some money and then they would go and have a gay time in the big city. Apparently I guess these were returning from a gay time in the city. They used to say that the train men couldn't understand them either but they thumped their hip pocket and if they felt a snooze scan or hit a snooze scan they threw them off at Troy. They were Scandinavians. Well, anyway, we arrived April 1st, 1912 in Troy and we stayed all night in the Greer Hotel which was located where Jamie Yarnits on that corner. There was a hotel there and we stayed there all night and Grifthorntz came down to get us the next day in the lumber wagon, practically hub deep in mud. That was Main Street at that time. Mud everywhere so we went and he was an oddball of a guy with a funny voice which we got quite a kick out of. He had a squeaky voice and he talked up real funny. But he took us up to the farm and there we were. The house still looks just like it did at that time. It may be a different color I'm not sure because it's had two or three coats of paint in the years but it's a big square house, two story house and sitting on an old and there it still sits. Only at that time when we got there the trees came clear down to the back of the house and there were trees. Out of the eighty acres I hate to say how many was in cultivation but the flat wasn't covered with trees, the meadow. That was about all it was and I think the rest was trees.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Did your father do the clearing then?

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: Yes, they cleared. They cut trees and cut brush and pile sticks and burned and we had a real good time doing it and hated every minute of it. I suppose, us kids, but since then I've learned to like that kind of work too. I thought it was real nice but all of us, we worked out there and cleared land. In 1931 the big fire went through out there and took the trees over across the creek. That has been cleared since there's a lot of, most of it's in cultivation now except one little section I guess still has trees. It doesn't look quite like it did. They left to grow back of the house for our picnics and things like that which we like very much to do. We raised carrots for the cows. My dad went into cows of course, jerseys.

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: He raised the cows. In fact, mother used to say that those cows she said would wade belly deep in water to get over to the pump where her dad would fill the tub for them. I'm sure they did. Anyway they had a barn. They built a barn. That was James Pence. His name was, he lived out there. Built a barn still standing sturdily. They claimed he was one of the best carpenters around. 1917 I believe the barn was built. Still got out there. I wish they'd put a nice coat of red paint on it. Make it look pretty. It was painted but it needs a new coat to be on.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Who owns the place now?

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: Melbourne Marsh.

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: Anyway while we were there we went to school and we were quite unhappy when we went to school because we had gone to the city school in Spokane and we thought we were quite smart. We got down here. Why they set us back a grade because we weren't up to where they were. That was a ball to our ego of course.

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: I think I was the fifth grader or sixth grader. I thought I'd made the sixth grade up there and I got to chuck back into the fifth. I think maybe the main reason was because they probably didn't have a sixth grade out there and they didn't need an extra grade. That's the way they did in the rural schools. I know we had a young teacher when I was supposed to have been in the seventh grade and she didn't have any other seventh graders so I was put in the eighth grade and missed the seventh grade which was not the thing to do because seventh grade lays the foundation for the eighth grade in those days. I had to take the eighth grade two years which was a blow also to my ego. Anyway, then we had about two miles to school I guess. We walked to school and we walked there across some ground. When the road was good and the weather was good enough, we walked and we did a lot of walking. In the winter when it was real bad and they did have snow, that first winter we were there was six feet on the level. That was hard to think. I remember how excited we were when that snow was on. We tunneled. We just had more fun in that snow. We never heard of such a thing before like that much snow. When it started to thaw in the spring, it crusted over and made crust. So it made the best coasting you could ever imagine but our first experience with it when it had crusted over, I remember yet how excited. We were almost in hysterics when we went out. The moon was shining so bright and we'd just gone out there. We found out we could walk on that snow. It was so hard and crusty. I can just remember my sister and I practically in hysterics, we just screamed because we could walk on the snow. We came in yelling at our parents, oh we can walk on the snow. Here we were. You can just imagine those two young kids galloping around out there and just looking at the snow because it was crusty. Then we found out it made wonderful coasting. I tried to even do some skiing but my sister and I didn't get too well along on our skis because our skis weren't trained. They wanted to go east and the other one west. North or south. You just can't get anywhere. We spent most of our time falling, having fallen down. I know the folks used to go out and coast with us. We had nice coasting hills. They'd go coasting too. That was fun. Anyway, back to our school.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Or should I mention about the creek going through the meadow?

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: We had a beautiful creek through there. One of these, it's still there when the water's high. There's still a creek there. It was lined with trees and bushes and it was beautiful. It had fish in it. We had a neighbor that had a boy our age and he just spent most of his time on that creek fishing. That was one of the pleasant memories that we have of that creek. We played in the water and we played along the creek. Just had bark boats and leaf boats and all kinds of boats that float down this creek and get the fish besides.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Does it still have fish?

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: After a while, they commenced to take the trees. One thing or another, in progress, I guess, the creek dried up in the summer and the fish were gone. So no more fishing and no more water in the summer. Eventually, they went to the source and took it to the towns using the water. I see. I see. Anyway, when we went to school, as I said, we had to walk to school. The weather was bad. My dad would hook up the team to the bobsled, which is the forerunners of the long sled. We'd wrap up blankets and cover our faces to keep the horse hair out of her mouth. The way we'd go for school, and he'd take us to school. Some of them, instead of taking their kids to school like that, they used to send them a horse. Someone would ride a horse to school and then when they'd get there, they'd turn the horse loose and it would go home. My dad decided rather than take us, he'd let us try it because we had an old gray horse there. He knew that she'd come home. So he parked the two of us on the horse and we had to go, our house was situated, we had to go this way. Then across the meadow and then follow the meadow on that side. There was no way going across it. So to stay on the road, we'd go like this. So he put us on the horse and we went down like this and we crossed the meadow. Then we got about halfway across the meadow on the other side, on the road on the other side of the meadow. I don't know why. That I can't remember why, but I think I was the one that for some reason rather slid off the horse. Rather I did it to see if I could get back on to practice or why. I do not know, but of course I couldn't get back on. We couldn't find a place, pulled the horse up. So I walked to school and my sister rolled the horse. I remember how angry my father was. He thought we were so dumb, we never got a chance to try it again. We carried our lunches. We didn't have hot lunches in those days, but I was talking to Elmer Vim over here in the rest home. He said, yeah, he said we carried it in lard buckets, ten pound lard buckets. I don't remember that I had a ten pound lard bucket because mine was about that high. I think it was a five pound lard bucket. We carried our lunches. The nearest to a hot lunch that we had was at Pro's on the way to school. We had to set it by the old big heater in there to thaw it out. So I guess that's what we had. So all the dinner buckets are sitting around the heater there to keep from freezing. If we'd have left them out in the anti -room or the coats were, they'd have frozen anyway, I guess. It was that cold. So we had to park around the heater. Anyway, that was, we didn't, we seemed to thrive on it. I said, well, another reason why those were nice buckets too, they made handy if the boys got fresh, why we could batter them on the head with those buckets. It didn't hurt anybody, but it served as a warning that they must not get too close. And eventually we did fall air. Somebody gave us a real treat and they'd got these tobacco cans that were quite long and shallow and had a lid that lifted up on a hinge. So we fell air to tobacco cans for our lunch buckets, which was a good day. We could arrange our lunch in there very neatly and pile it up instead of having it all poked in one bucket. So we thought that was a real treat to have a large tobacco can for a lunch box. When the weather was good and the roads were good, some of the kids rode bicycles to school. And I tried desperately to learn to ride a bicycle. I remember there was one boy that I kind of liked pretty well. He had a girl's bicycle too and he let me try it. But after putting my foot through the spokes and things like that, falling off a few times, he gave it up to the man, Joe. I guess he was glad too. I can't remember just how we had water when we came out to the farm. But I remember one of the first things that they did was to drill a well. We had a well right by the house. It had a pump in it. But they had drilled quite deep and then there was sand down in there, something in that caved in. So we never had a very deep well and never had water enough really for, you know, except house shoes. And they had, and of course the creek went through, so they had water for the, because we had a creek down across our place, another place too there. And it was kind of a spring apparently. It stayed water in it all the time for the cattle. And then they were down in the flat where the creek went, so they had water that way. Eventually the folks decided that they would put a cistern, well they had put an orchard up on the hill then where they had cleared land and raised fruit up there, raspberries. Had a real lovely garden, everything just about you could imagine, raised garden. And so they built us, or dug us, made a cistern up there and dug a well down by the creek and piped it up to the cistern. So then we had piped water into the house and we went modern. Before that we had these little outhouses out behind. We weren't modern people then, we had to travel out in the weather. But anyhow, they put in a bathroom, we had a bathtub, and we had indoor water. Hot and cold, they put in a hot water tank, so we had hot cold water. And they had water in us so they could irrigate and help the gardens. And that was a big boost too. And we were very happy with that till...

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: What else do you want? Did you help your mother around the house a lot?

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: Yes I did.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Did you mostly help your father?

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: Well, I did both. In fact, I remember that we took turns doing dishes and I had the breakfast dishes, my sister had the supper dishes, and we usually fought over the noon dishes. We were supposed to have taken turns and I guess we did pretty well on that one. And I learned to make bread and must have done pretty well, I've forgotten how since for some reason or other. But anyway, I used to make bread and I liked to iron. My mother hated to iron. And so she said that worked out just right. She didn't mind the washing, but I remember I did the washing too. And that was in the days when we had a little washing machine that had the thing that you grabbed it and run it back and forth like this, like two washboards. They were rounded like this and then you'd rub it back and forth like that. Then we had a ringer, she had to ring out by hand. We set it outside on the ground and we washed. We had a hydrant out there so we could have cold water. We had to heat the water in the house to begin with. We heated it in a boiler. In fact, my mother I think usually boiled quite close, so we always heated a boiler full of water. That's how we got our hot water to wash, even though our stove had a reservoir. So we had hot water there too, but we had to heat extra water when we wanted to wash. We washed and hung our clothes out on the line. Then we had, for some reason, whether it seemed like we had quite a bit of ironing. I remember I used to like to iron. Where I lost that flavor. I don't like to iron though. But anyway, then we had to clean our own rooms and clean the stairs.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: What did you do with clothes in the wintertime when you had to wash?

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: They froze dry. They went out on the line. They went outside anyway. Except when we bring them in, we had clothes dryers so we maybe have to finish them up like that. Leave them out there longer. They freeze dry. It was cold hanging them out though, for sure.

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: Really, I preferred working outdoors, so I did work with my dad a great deal. In fact, I helped milk and helped in the hay. I drove the horse on the hay fork and helped load the hay too. I rode the hay load. My dad would throw it on. I'd spread it out on there. I did that for a long time until finally it seemed to be the Timothy got me. I had the hay fever so terrible that I had to quit. That was a sad day. I didn't like having to quit in the hay because I liked it. We had to weed. I guess maybe the thing I hated the worst of all, the folks a couple or two or three years decided to raise carrots for the cows. They raised stock carrots, great big carrots. We had to get on our hands and knees and weed and thin the carrots. I remember we just groused and howled and complained about that. So, finnishly, finally the folks gave up the carrots. I didn't like the cows like them. Oh yeah, I don't know. Maybe it made the milk creamier. I don't know, but it was supposed to be good. Maybe it improved their eyesight like it's supposed to be. So, you could find more grass. I forgot to tell you about the time that my younger, the little girl, when we first came there, we were down there playing in the meadow. Okay, I'm going to have to turn this over first. Okay.

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: Okay. See, we had her along with us. That was when we first came there. She was about a year and a half old, I suppose. We had her with us. And it went down to the stream that was in the meadow at the time. We were just playing around down there. We came to this one place that was too wide for her to get across, but we seemed to be able to manage to get across. So, we got across and we couldn't reach her to reach her across, or she wouldn't cooperate or somehow or other. Anyway, we got the bright idea of tossing her over. And my sister got a hold of her and threw her over to me, and I missed her. She fell in the water. And of course, it was deep enough in that spot, so I guess she went under. Anyway, it didn't hinder her valor -ing though. She could be heard clear all over the district, I'm sure. She had a little so hard. Mother was up there at the house, and she heard her. She couldn't help but hear her. Anyway, she came down to rescue her. No, she didn't come down. She started down, but meanwhile, we grabbed her out of the water and started her home, her home dripping wet. And of course, we were kind of scared because we were responsible for having been in the water. But we figured since she was making that much noise, she couldn't be too bad off. But I think she kind of still holds it against us to this day. She said, I know you tried to drown me.

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: Before I got done with it, maybe I had, I don't know.

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: We had the oil, and that's when I got my first rifle too. I just looked forward to the day when I could have a rifle, just like the kids of today, apparently. And I got a rifle and went out to shoot squirrels. There were lots of squirrels in those days. We trapped, that's how we made money. My dad gave me a penny or two for every squirrel we could catch. And so we got, then I got the rifle. I went out to shoot the squirrels. But my aim was not too very good. For some reason or other, I'd probably wind up throwing sticks at the squirrels to make them go in the hole to shut up. It could be, you know. But then I decided I was going to go hunting. And we had grouse and those days out there in the pheasants, wild in the woods, you know. And I went out and I shot one. And then, in fact, I shot two. And I had to run them down. And I felt so sorry for them. I never went hunting again. We ate them. But I decided that wasn't my need to put them in the fight hunt. But the squirrels, I didn't mind if I could have shot the squirrels. I did shoot some, but they laughed at me mostly, I think, because they knew I was a poor shot. Oh, we played, too. Then we had them. I never did tell you about the dog we got. That was way back, though. That was after we came here. We always had a dog go. I went one for one week or the other. And he'd run down the hill with us and we'd be coasting. And he just loved to go coasting. He didn't ride coasting, but he'd run up and down the hills, ride all the way with us. I think we had more fun coasting. And that was just loads of fun. And then my sister and I, we had imaginations throughout this world is about. We played. We had what we call fancy ladies that grew along the creek. They'd be big like this and flouncey skirts. We had hollyhocks. We'd make hats for them. And these fancy ladies were our playthings. And the hollyhocks, we made things out of the hollyhocks. And we made our playthings. We didn't have fancy playthings. We didn't have money to buy things. I don't believe we even had things to buy. That I remember, we didn't seem to miss them, because we made our own fun and paper dolls. Catalogs. We cut paper dolls out by the 100. And we fixed our own. We fixed our houses. We played outdoors. And we'd fix regular houses and have our paper dolls. We'd have different families. And they'd go visiting each other and everything. And my sister especially had an imagination that was just hard to believe. She could just make regular stories. I wish I had some of them that she made right now. But then I got older. Why she and my younger sister, they did it. And I was kind of out then. I don't know if I wanted to be here or if I was just naturally the third one. Anyway, I was the one that wasn't on the imagination playing. But my sister was real good to play with the younger one. And I wasn't so good, apparently. She and I didn't get it off too good.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: What did your father do with his milk from the dairy?

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: We had a separator and separated the cream from the milk. And he sold the cream. And fed the milk to the pigs and the calves. And to us. Because we made cottage cheese. And we used milk in cooking a great deal. And the cream, we churned our own butter. Many a time I remember running the old churn up and down, making butter. And my mother used milk and cream. She had eggs too because she had chickens. And the eggs were her pin money. And the cream was my dad's. So we had chickens. And that raised our own chickens to eat. She sold the eggs. And he sold the cream. Caves raised her own beef. And they had, usually, I can't remember if we had, I think they'd get a pig or two and raise that. So it's to have their, and they smoked it themselves. They had a little building made. And so they smoked through on me.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Do you remember what that looked like? A little? The building? A small house?

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: Yeah, it looked like a little outdoor toilet. It was right out behind the woodshed. They built a fire down in the bottom on the ground. Hickory smoke or whatever kind. Apple trees made good smoke, too, I remember. And then they closed it up. It was quite tight. And they closed it up and keep the fire going a certain number of days. So just to smoke it when they got done, it made just really super smokey.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: And they do that just with pork, or would they use other meat?

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: Oh, they just did the pork, as I remember. The beef.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: How did they keep the beef?

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: I think Mother canned beef. Yeah, she did. She canned it. Because they had no way of keeping it, otherwise. We had a cellar underneath the house, which was always cold. And they kept their milk and cream and things down in that cellar. And their canned fruit, canned vegetables, canned meat. Just had a real supply of all kinds of food that way. After working real hard at it, I was there. I marvel how these country women could get as much done as they did, really. And the supply thing, it's like that. Nowadays, they have your refrigerators and your automatic, this, that, and the other thing.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: No automatics then, right?

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: And yet, I think maybe we had more fun. I remember having the dead down the tree right close to the house there. And it had a pine squirrel nest in it. It had several little pine squirrels. And I remember I adopted two of them. I guess the others were killed, maybe, when it fell. They were cute little pets. And they got sick. And I doped one of them with castor oil. And the other one, I gave them other crows, because they had diarrhea. And one of them died, but I don't remember which one it was, if it was the one that got the castor oil or the mother crows. But anyway, one of them didn't survive. And the other one did. And I had it there until my mother said I'd have to do something about that squirrel, because he started gnawing the stair steps.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Oh, he ran loose in the house?

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: Oh, yeah. Oh, yes. And his special place was me. He thought I was a tree trunk.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: And those two little squirrels with scamper, they'd run up my skirt, see?

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: And around and around and around, they'd go up on my shoulder, up on my head. And when they'd play until they got tired, and then they'd each one climb into one of my pockets. And I'd walk around with those two little squirrels asleep in my pockets. And they were just adorable. But finally, after the one died, and then the second one, of course, was getting bigger all the time. Why, then he started to have the run of the house. And his place of chewing was the stair steps, which did not ameep with anyone's approval for some reason or other. So I had to fix a cage for him. And it was fun to watch, because they always wash everything before they eat it. You give them a pine cone, and they go soak it in their water dish before they'll work at it. Work at it. And they did have a pine cone. Or a cone soaking in the water dish most of the time. But anyway, then I was going to... This was after I got old enough to teach. And I had this squirrel and some other inform me that she wasn't going to take care of that squirrel. I'd have to do something about that squirrel before I left. So I took it out in the woods. Each day, I'd take it out there and turn it loose. It would run back to me. And day after day, finally I went off and it ran up in a tree. And I went home. The next day, I'd go out and whistle at it. And it would come scamper and play and play and play. Crawl into my lap and go to sleep. And I did that day after day. And finally, it got a little more weary all the time. And I'd have to whistle or holler to it a little harder, longer. And it would come up through the tree tops. And still it would come chik, chik, chik, you know, try to... A little bit bashful. Till it would come and then it discovered who it was. And then we'd play and curl my lap and go to sleep. And finally, one day it didn't come.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Had something happened to it? Had it gone wild?

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: I never know. What the squirrel was going.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: You told me too last time about feeding mice to an owl that somebody had?

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: Oh yes. This man that we bought the place from. He was a sort of a queer guy and he played with a wagon, even though he was a full -grown man. But he had a bachelor or he had moved into a house farther down the road. And he got a little owl. And this was the cutest little thing. We used to go watch him twist his head off, but he never did. Yeah, they'll turn their head. And we gave it mice. It loved mice and it would swallow the mouse whole. And then after a while it would start to twist its neck and stretch itself, you know. And regurgitate, regurgitate, whatever you call that word. And this mouse, the skin and the feet would come out. And the rest would stay down to how he separated it. I'll never know, but that was it. And so we used to like to take mice to watch that owl eat the mice.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Did they have to be live or did he eat them dead? Did he eat them dead?

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: Oh, he picked them dead. We had them dead, of course, when we took them. They weren't going to carry no live mice there. No, they were dead when he got them. But he was a cute little thing, a little barn owl, I guess it was. That was his pet. Of course, we wished we had the barn owl too, but we didn't. But this Melvin Marsh that bought our place, he was just a kid at the time too. He had a ground squirrel for a pet. It hibernated. They had root cellars.

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: We pitted the potatoes too, by the way. That's how we kept potatoes. You pack them with straw down in the ground. You dig in there. Then you put a lot of it over top and then you cover it with dirt. And when a carrot and potatoes fold that way. Then when you wind them while you just dig in and drag some out. They had a regular root cellar, they called it, because it had been made with timber, apparently. Then they had it covered with dirt over there. This ground squirrel hibernated in that. He dug a hole and stayed there all winter. Then the springy came out. We wished we had one like that, so we could have had one too. I was wondering if you'd tell me some more about the school and the programs you put on. The different types of things. Yeah, that was in those days we fixed our own.

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: It was a rural school, of course. We had all the way from one to eight grades in a room. And the one teacher, she had all the way from five -year -olds on up to 14 -year -olds in the same room. She'd have one grade come up to her desk. She'd hear whatever it was they were having, whether it was reading or spelling or arithmetic. She'd make an assignment and send them back to their seat. We had double seats, so two sat at a desk. Then she'd have the next class come up. She'd spend just a few minutes with that class. Molly had whatever twice she had called up for them to do. She'd make an assignment and send them back to their seats and the next one would come. It went all day. We'd have our morning recess when we went out and played games like pump, pump, pull away.

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: If you don't come, I'll pull you away.

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: One group would be over there. This one would come over and they'd say, pump, pump, pull away. They'd try to tempt them away from their base. If they could get them out, they'd catch them. If they could catch a whole of them, then they had to join the other group, so whichever one would gather all of us on this side over to this side, this is the winning side. They'd divide up the side to say, pump, pump, pull. Kind of like a tag. Yeah.

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: One of our favorites then, because there were lots of trees and hiding places around there, was called Run, Sheep, Run. That we'd divide up to in two groups.

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: All right, here's Ty.

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: For this one, when we'd do this one, we'd have captains for each side. This one would meet with his group and he'd give them colors. Each color would mean something and this one would do the same with his group. One group would take his—this was Run, Sheep, Run. The others were the sheep, see. The captain would take his sheep out and hide in some place.

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: After they were hidden, the captain would come back and say, my sheep are hidden. This other group would go out, but they also had to stay with the captain. You couldn't scatter out. You'd go as a group because the captain would lead his sheep. They'd go around the seep. They could find where this other one had hid his sheep. If he was getting close to him, why maybe he'd say, green, green. Watch out, they'd go over a yellow or a blue or whatever. The colors were decided on. He'd try, of course, in doing it. See how my voice is going. He'd try to get his sheep into base without their being caught by this other group. If he got them close enough to base so he thought they could make it, and this other group had got far enough away, why, he'd tell her. The color formed to go in, see. Might be red, red, run, sheep, run. Then they rushed to get there. Of course, these others, they'd tear to try to beat them to it. We really just had a lot of fun with that one. It was quite exciting. Then the pump pump pull away. It seemed like there was another one. All the steel sticks that they'd put the sticks out in the middle. They'd come up there and they'd try to grab a stick, see, and without being caught by the other side. The one that could get them old sticks would win.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: This other one, what did they call that one?

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: Anyway, they ran across. Well, I guess that's what they did. One was out in the middle. They'd run across and this one in the middle would try to catch one. Then they'd catch one, then the two out in the middle. They would try to catch them when they traded places. Oh. Pretty soon they'd probably have them all out in the middle. They'd see which one had the most left. If they'd got them all from one side, this one might still have one. That'd be the winning side.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: What did you do in the wintertime when you couldn't go outside? Or did you go outside anyway?

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: We had snowball fights just like everybody else. If it was too bad to be out, one of the things that we quite often had was spell downs. We loved to have spell downs inside and clap in, clap out. To do that, you'd sit in the desk and somebody would be it. They'd come and they'd sit there. If you were perfectly willing to let them, you didn't clap. But if you didn't want them, you'd clap. They had to get to go someplace else. I can't just exactly remember what the object was. It was to keep them clapping, clap out. We'd let them in. Sometimes we'd let them in and sometimes we wouldn't. They'd go and try to find a place where they could. There must have been somebody trying to catch them. Meanwhile, I guess that was it. I'd have two out if they got caught before they got a place that somebody would let them sit down while they had the VM.

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: I remember that was— Musical chair. You probably know what that is. We played that too. I remember we were in our desks.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Did you have a record player or did somebody sing?

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: Well, yes, we did. We had a record player. Old -fashioned one too. It must have been. You had to grind and crank it to keep it going.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: You said you had a lot of singing?

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: That was one of our opening. We did. We had quite a bit of singing. In our programs, we memorized pieces. When we'd have a program, everybody had a piece. We had a dialogue. Our dialogues, that was the one. Everybody enjoyed that, I think. They'd put on regular little plays, little dialogues they called them, for little, sharp plays. We'd have dialogues and recitations and songs. I don't know if anybody was ever smart enough to own a musical instrument. The plays at Mount Hortons, and the community, they had, well, they'd call them lyceums now. I suppose they did, but we didn't exactly. Maybe they called it a lyceum. Anyway, the community would get together. They'd write up a newspaper. I still have one that Ed wrote up. He and I were in the school. They'd have drives on people, jokes. They were really quite clever. I should hunt up mine so you can read our one that Ed and I put on one time. Paper, newspaper. Anyway, that was it. They had basket socials. And to do that, why, the women would fix a basket of food. And they had auction sale. They'd sell the baskets to the highest bidder. They'd have to eat supper with whoever's basket they got. It was kind of popular. Somebody wanted to eat supper with you. He had to keep bidding. If they wanted to eat supper with someone, they had to bid pretty high. The fellas knew who brought the basket. They weren't supposed to, but occasionally, we were pretty suspicious that somebody did.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: What did they use the money for?

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: I suppose the library and things like that. They must have.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: That how did they get the libraries?

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: Because we didn't have a lot of money then. But they turned it in for something, I suppose. Oh, yes. Then we had our tin buckets that we drank out of at school. We didn't have water in the school. We had a pump that we had to go out and pump the water. We'd bring it in in a tin bucket and set it on a shelf. And they had a tin bucket or a dipper.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: And anybody wanted to get a drink?

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: You go and you take a dipper full of water and you drink it. And if you were nice, you dumped it in a bucket. And if you weren't, you just had to put it back in the bucket. I saw them do that too. But anyway, that went on for quite a long time. And then maybe they might have used the money to buy that. They got a big container for water. And it had a little faucet thing. And you could go. And then they came to the point where we were supposed to have our own drinking cups. So that was in the days. They had a little folding drinking cup. So we had a little folding drinking cup in our dinner bucket. And we'd stretch out our little folding drinking cup and go and get our own water in our own. That was, of course, that was quite an improvement over the old fashioned tin dipper in the tin bucket. You could imagine. And we burned wood in the heaters in the stove where it had nothing else but wood to burn. Let's see.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: What else did we have? What were your books like?

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: Our books. Well, we didn't have fancy books like they had now. In fact, when I take up a second grade reader now and look at it, I marvel that second graders could read the books. When you think of what they have now, or the first grade readers even, too. And they were much more advanced than what they are now. We did not have any Jane and Dick books and Dick and Jane books. They just started out reading adult type books. Yes, they did. They did a primary letter. And our spelling, we were, I remember we had little red spelling books. About like that. And they were about that thick. And we had spelling words. And we had certain ones. And they weren't all fixed up to have fancy notebooks and workbooks. The workbooks were done by us on our scratch paper. We didn't have fancy paper either. We had regular tablets and pencils. And we used that then. We had to do our own sentence structure and everything. And when we had to take a test, we didn't have no true and false tests either. We had to write out the answers. And when we got through the eighth grade, we had to come to Troy and take our eighth grade examinations. And the Troy school, there were state tests. And we had to pass those. Our teacher couldn't be there. No other teacher could be there either. But it was up to us alone. And I remember how scared we were. We weren't used to coming to town. We rarely came to town. That was such a rare occasion. I guess maybe it was a treat. I don't know. Anyway, we were traveling with horses and buggies and things. You didn't need to go to town for our entertainment. Each community was really a separate thing by itself. Our number work, too. We didn't have fancy workbooks in our number books. We had problems assigned. And the teacher would show us how to do it. And we went down to our desks and we worked them out. They weren't done anywhere near for us. We had just played in the little old number books full of problems. We were independent workers. I think that was when one of the things people used to— then they started talking consolidation. They should consolidate to give the rural people more improvement over better education and things of that kind. And they consolidated, figured they could get the money from the rural districts, I guess, to help the town. But it was kind of funny. The ones that were the valedictorian and the slutatorians were these kids that came from the rural districts. They were independent workers. They didn't have to have their work candy -coated for them because they had been assigned and sent to their desk to work it out. And our library wasn't stupendous like it is now. I suppose we just had one little old library, as I remember, with a certain number of books. Our teachers read to us in the mornings. They'd read stories to us. I guess they do that, too, now. We'd have singing if the teacher could sing. Apparently, they could share the time because I remember we did have singing. And we had singing in our programs. I don't remember if we had any musical instruments there particularly.

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: I thought, Maddy, you could tell us a little more about the community and going to the school and how important it was.

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: They were all working more together. They did. They had, of course, the women met. They had their dinners and they had their coffee parties and things. I suppose maybe somewhat like they do around. There are, I'm glad to see, that the communities, after a while, they realized that they had lost their social life. Many of the communities have gone back now and cooperated in working together to have a social life of their own. Like the Big Meadow Club. This is when they lost their schools. Yeah. After they lost their school, they were just at a standstill. They were lost because they didn't want to go to town. They weren't accepted in town. They had no interest in hobnobbing with the city people. Yet they had no school to hold them together, to call them to have a thing, a program. There was a while there that everything just kind of stood at a standstill.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Finally, one community after another said, well, why should we just die as a community?

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: We're still here. Let's have our own social life. One by one, it seemed like the communities had done that. I know the Big Meadow out there, they didn't have a place exactly that they could gather. Then the women formed the Big Meadow Club and they got together. They would meet, I guess maybe it was once a month, they had a meeting. They got together and had a social life. Now, like this year, I see they're having a quilt that they're going to raffle at Community Day. They used to have picnics. They get together for picnics and the Big Meadow Club. They do. They have family picnics now. Once or twice a year. Christmas, they have a gathering. In the summer, they usually have a picnic or two. They have kind of gathered their own Big Meadow. I mean, there's school Ridge out there. Burnt Ridge has a friendly Friday club. They meet and they do the same thing. They get their families together once or twice a year too, I think. This is kind of why the little school was so important. Everybody met there. They met there and their children went to school there. The teacher was instrumental in gathering the community together for their little programs. Eventually, their little programs were not only just the kids. They had the literary society. The people in the community had their program. If the community was the kind that played cards, they'd get together and they'd play cards. If they were the kind that had dances, they had dances. The Big Meadow Club out there, or the Big Meadow District, they, as I recall, they didn't play cards. Well, the school was too small, really, I suppose. All they played rook. Yes, they did. They had a game called a rook. Eventually, that was what they'd do because the men were interested in that. Then they seemed like after the schoolhouse had been burned down too. After they had the new one built, I don't know if they ever met in that one exactly for that, but they'd meet in the homes and they'd have their own parties. Before, when the schoolhouse was still there, they were having there until the kids were sent to town. They were bused to town and that just kind of left them holding the sack out there. They didn't have anything to do.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: When did the consolidation come about?

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: I can't just remember. That I've forgotten a year for that one, but it's been many years, of course. You were telling me last time when we talked about a game called Poor Pussy.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: About what?

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: Poor Pussy. Yes. That was described as that. I had that one too. Somebody would be it and they'd have to go kneel in front of somebody. Very pleading, they'd say, Poor Pussy. Meow, meow. The one that they knelt was to say, Poor Pussy and Pat, that one. The Pussy cat, of course, was trying to make the one that he was kneeling in front of laugh. If he could make that one laugh, they had to trade places. Some of them went around and the meowing that they did was out of this world, I must say. It took quite a bit of a stamina to withstand them and still not laugh.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Is this one that the parents played too?

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: Yeah, I guess they did. Especially the young people. Of course, they played post office. Remember that one. And games like Who's Got the Key, they'd have a string. They'd put a string around and everybody would stand in a circle and pass the key on this string. And the one in the center would try to guess who had it in their hand. They'd just keep it passing. It just kept working like this. Of course, the key was traveling all the time. And the one in the center would try to guess when a certain person got it, who it was.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: If they guessed it, why didn't they get to trade places?

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: And then some of the communities, they like to play these old folk games like Virginia Rio and Gust of Skull.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: What's Gust of Skull?

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: Well, that was just loads of fun. They had to have Gust of Skull. Let me think. I got to go back and think on that one myself. But it was an old folk dance game. Sounds like it was Scandinavian. Yeah, it was. And the partners, two partners, Gust of Skull. Formed a square, like a square. And they'd sing and go into each other back and around. And Virginia Rio, of course, they had the two long rows, you know, how that one goes, I guess.

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: Most of the communities approved of dancing then. No, I not bonafide dancing. Some of them did not approve of it. I remember when I was growing up, my folks did not. My father disapproved of card playing and dancing both. But we'd go to the literary society. And then when they started shoving the benches back to dance, we went home.

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: But they danced. Some of those dances were quite gay, you know, like the Black Bob and the Charleston. Oh, they did those. Yeah, they did those. But we never did. We didn't even get to stay and watch them very much. When they started in on that, why we went home. You mentioned, too, about being how proud of your mother when she did a reading. Oh, yes. They'd have different ones. Well, I think partly they were volunteered, you know, to have a part in the program or they were supposed to give one. And I remember my mother getting up. She had been a former teacher and she could memorize like nothing. Seemed like she'd memorize this and get up there and give it in a dramatic way, too. Not too far -fetched, a dramatic, but she would give a very good reading. And I remember how proud we were when mother would get up to give her a reading. She always did a beautiful job of it. No matter what she attempted to do, she did. She was capable.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Were they mostly Scandinavian people out in Big Meadow?

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: Or a lot of them were.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Was there any hard feelings or difficulties getting into a community?

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: Not that I recall. There wasn't too much there because they were all practically of the same group. There was some jealousy among them if there was some.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: No, I mean, as an outsider coming into a Scandinavian community, did your family have problems?

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: No, we didn't. Not that I recall. In fact, they thought my father was a Norwegian, so he was taken in with open arms.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Even with an English name, huh?

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: I have to call it Cartwright's son, I guess.

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: We felt quite proud of the fact that he, in a way, because we were in a Scandinavian community, they were all, I think, just about all, a lot of them were Scandinavian. So we,

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: well, on second thought, yes, I believe they had friends, but they weren't all. So there was. I remember now that my sister, her feelings were hurt more than once because she had gone out to work and there were Scandinavians. The others were Scandinavians. And they would talk their language and leave her out. They were inclined to do that. That was the one thing. They didn't have good manners. I remember there was a criticism of that. I'd forgotten about that. But you'd go in there and here they would be talking there, Sweden or region, and they didn't care if we were there. The folks were there. They did it in front of everybody. They'd just carry on their own conversation. Then you could be left out in the cold and they could care less.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Did they do it on purpose or was it just a slip on their part?

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: It was just, they talked that. I don't think they did it on purpose. I would hope that they didn't. Although my sister was sure that those girls did. So she'd go working along there. They were hoying, I think. And she was sure that they probably were talking about her. I don't know if they were. No one could know because nobody could understand what they were talking about.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Was there any trouble in school with them adapting to English?

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: Not with us when we went to school. We had no trouble that I recall of that at all.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: When the youngsters came in, did they speak English?

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: Yeah.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: I recall there were none that came in, but what could speak English into the school?

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: In that school. One time I was to another school. That was when we were up in the Valley, Valley, Washington. There was an Austrian family moved in there. The kids came to school and they couldn't talk English at all. They came and I remember they were our neighbors.

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: The father talked. He was broken English, but he asked us, Alvin and I, if we'd kind of watch over them. I remember how blank the teacher looked when she tried to get through to them and find out their names and one thing or another about them. They couldn't understand her and she couldn't understand them.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: I remember looking at us and said, can you find out for us?

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: I remember just how helplessly she was appealing for help on finding out. They must have gotten so they could talk English before too long because I remember the father saying, oh, Tony, she's doing pretty good, but Annie, he's having more trouble. And it struck us so funny because he got his pronouns all balled up. But out here in the middle, there was a mixture out there. There was a family out there who was strictly English and besides us. Quite a few of them were Scandinavian. But when they'd get together, I remember that the folks were quite perturbed because they would talk their language. And I guess one reason why they did was because that was their native tongue and they could express themselves better that way. And they were, I think, thoughtless and just maybe kind of poor mannered. If I remember rightly, I believe we decided that they didn't have very good manners because they, right in front of everybody, they'd talk that language and they made no effort to try to translate what they were talking about either. There was only a few that seemed to have a little better reading or something. They would do that. But as a rule, I don't remember if my folks, in a way,

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: their standards were different, should I say. I don't know just exactly.

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: If they stood for something, they stood for it and they held fast. There were no hands to sell out on what they believed and what they wanted to do. And I remember they're getting kind of discouraged with some of their neighbors because they were more on the wishy -washy type. And if somebody would come with a better story, they'd peel out. I remember my dad being kind of disgusted about that. Taking it all in all, the younger generation, I don't remember if we had too much trouble. Probably we were just like the rest of them. I don't know. Anyway, we seemed to have lots of fun and didn't think too much about it. You were telling me about the importance of May baskets before. Oh, yes. I counted that down. Oh, that was one of the highlights of the year, was May Basket Day. And we just really went to town and made fancy May baskets. And we fixed them up and had candy and flowers in them. We did. We'd take them out and deliver them. And there was a couple old bachelors lived up on the hill. And so my sister and I decided that that would be fun if we could get up there and deliver them a May basket, too. And there was quite a ways from the house, from our house. But we went over there with our May basket and took it to the door and fear and trembling. I can just hear my heart pounding yet. I don't know what we thought would happen if we ever did get caught. But we sure didn't intend to be caught. And they had a lumber wagon sitting out in front of the house. Otherwise, there was nothing to hide us. If we hadn't hidden, we would have been wide open to the wide open spaces. They could see us because there was a long road down there from the house. So we had to hide. But fortunately, I guess they didn't know what it was all about. So we hid this May basket, knocked on the door, and then we ran and hid under this wagon and watched. And we were just fit to be tied. We wanted to giggle some air. And they came to the door and they got it. I can just see their gears meshing, wondering what it was all about. I imagine. They had never found out. But they didn't try to find us. They never, I'm sure they never found out who hid them after they went in the house while we took off from home. We had our fun.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Were they Scandinavian bachelors that didn't date?

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: So they wouldn't have known what an A basket was.

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: We got a kick out of it anyhow. But we used to go and hide. There would be quite a ways around, too. We'd have to go and hang our May baskets. But that was quite one of the highlights of the year, was May baskets. Well, let's see. Another highlight of the year is Christmas.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Did you have anything special at Christmas time that your family used to do?

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: Oh, yeah. We almost made a lot at Christmas, of course. And then, of course, we were coming to church in Troy at that time. We came down and they always had a Christmas tree and program at the church. And then the school, of course, always had a Christmas program.

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: They had a real program there. So we – and for our family, we didn't have lots of costly gifts like they have now, believe me. We were happy if Mother had slipped around on the side and made us something to wear or something like that, mainly, that I remember. And if we ever did get a toy or a game, it would be more have to be a game of some kind. Why, that was really cherished because the few things that we got were nice. And then they always had – one of the things we always had was a Christmas dinner. We had plum pudding. I still make it. I started in – it was an English plum pudding, but I don't put the brandy over and burn it, but it's suet pudding. Takes three, four hours to boil it. And that was one of our highlights. And we usually had a chicken. And then when the folks raised turkeys, why we even broadened out, they had a turkey. They raised turkeys for a while. They raised turkeys. They did that for two or three years or three, four years. But the owls would come and the coyotes would come. It was kind of bad because the woods had come so close to the house that we had quite a bit of trouble with losing chickens from those. And then there was a small varmint, a weasel, I guess, would come in, too. Another thing that the folks had trouble with was a beaver. The beaver would be in the creek and they'd dam it up so that they'd flood the meadow. And I remember the folks didn't like that. Finally, I guess they had a county bed.

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: Get the trap beaver and move them to elsewhere.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Oh, this is like when? What year? What year are we talking about? With the beavers?

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: This was while we were still at home. Of course, the owls was at home, I guess, except when I was out of school and teaching. Okay, you were telling me about you went to high school and you had to move into Troy. You waited for your sister and you both moved into Troy.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Was this a common thing for kids in the country to move into town and go to high school?

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: They would come in and batch. Get a room in somebody's home. There were quite a few of them that did it that way. And I remember we did, too. And in the winter when it was too bad so we couldn't go back and forth, we walked back and forth a lot. Three miles. And during the winter when the weather was bad and then the folks would rent a little room for us and put in a little stove and the nearest things to have. And we'd carry food from home to prepare and we'd stay down there, but just during school days. Home weekdays. Weekends. So really there was a lot of freedom for teenagers then. I know when we came like that, I just really marvel now when I see how things have changed in regard to kids getting on together, boys and girls, I suppose. Because there was my sister and I and we batched in this little room and we studied. That's what we did. We fixed our food and we studied. We didn't have all the musical instruments and things like that. And when we got bored and didn't have anything to do, we'd tear out from. We'd even walk out after school sometimes when the weather wasn't so good. I suppose if we ran out of food especially or we wanted a good meal, why we'd take off or our studies weren't so bad. And we did high school in three years then instead of four. And when we got done with high school, then we, the folks had told us that they couldn't afford to educate us farther than high school. If we wanted to get further education, it would be up to us. So I said, well, one thing I knew I didn't want to do, I didn't want to teach, but I had in mind then I thought I'd like to be a telegrapher, learn telegraphing, work in the depot or something, or something else, anything but teaching. But the only thing that seemed to be open was to go to summer school in Lewiston. So my dad gave me $25. I guess he gave my sister $25. That's what we started out. That was a loan. And we went to school, I suppose it was six weeks to summer school. The hottest summer I'm sure Lewiston ever had. That was in 19, 19, was 19, 19, yeah, 19, 19.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Wow, was that a hot one?

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: Even the enamel on the bedstead melted. I know. After we finished that one, we taught first year school. I had that on Driscoll Ridge here, biggest school I've had all the time, just about, I guess. Scared to death. I went out there and I was, I was, Maudie Helm's mother was on the school board and she was an old teacher, believe me, and she was keeping close track of things. I was scared to death of her, had a right to be. I was so green, it was pitiful. But anyway, I remember having trouble out there.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: I told Mother, I said, what'll I do?

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: I just can't handle them. So she came out and visited school one day and she said, oh, I can tell you what I do. That big kid, about the next time he did something and I'd tell him what to do. And if he didn't do it, I'd say, go home. On that other one, she said, that's causing all that trouble. I'd warn him and if he didn't do what he was supposed to do, I'd give him a lickin'. So the next time this big one got up and wouldn't sit down and was sassin' and talkin' out loud, I said, pick up your books and go home. Either sit down and behave yourself or pick up your books and go home. And I had to sit down and tell him because my knees were knocking together so hard, I was so scared. But he picked up his books and he went out and he threw something, a rock, I guess, at the school door when he left. That was the last I saw of him. And there, I don't know how soon after that, this other kid got smarting off and so I had him stay after school and I gave him a lickin'. Got along pretty good after that. Decided she knew what she was talkin' about. I didn't go back there the next year. I had no desire. In fact, when I started out to teach, I said, I'm not gonna teach more than one year in a school. I wanna see how many schools I can teach in before I quit. And so I, oh, and then as soon as that school was over, I went back to summer school again. I believe it was nine weeks then. And after the nine weeks, I got another school. I wanted to stay close enough home so when I could always come home weekends, my dad would hook up the team and take my little trunk and away we'd go. He'd move me to where I was gonna board.

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: And I guess that one place, though he didn't, I was up by place that one time. The school up there. I don't think he took me there. That was quite a way, so I must've gone some other way that time. But all these others. When I'd come and get me weekends, it was bring me home, take me back on Sunday. Teach, he was real good about that. Vomitable horse driver, but he scared me to death. But we made it.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: He drive too fast?

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: No, he'd just let the lines loose and the horses would go where they want to. And I was sure we'd go off the edge of the bank. I mean, never did. But anyway, I went to summer school about three summers, I guess, and was taught like that. Then I decided that I managed to pay back my $25 and had some money put by. So I was going to go a whole year and just have us to be a part of the social life of the group and have fun, because I wanted to have my picture in the annual and I wanted them to say something about me in the annual like they did there. So I did that. I signed up for the whole term that time. And but mid -term, my dad called me and wondered if I'd be willing to come out and teach in the east and had to leave. And he wondered if I'd be willing to quit and come out and teach. Well, I kind of hated to, but I did. And my younger sister was in the eighth grade at the time. So here I came. And of course, she was my sister. And there were three of them in the eighth grade. Ed's younger, one of Ed's sisters was there and Clarence Ferguson were three of them. And the two girls were pretty good. They worked, but Clarence, he was not doing so well. And I worked those kids.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Did I ever work those kids?

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: To this day, Hester said I was the meanest teacher she ever had. But she passed. And so did Alice and so did Clarence. I know when they went to take the test, they were still having to go down and take the test on it. Troy, I said, well, if Clarence will just be fortunate enough to sit by somebody that knows what they're doing, even he might make it. He'd copy, you mean. Copy. I said, that's the only way he'll ever stand a chance, but he passed. This is that old schoolhouse out here, of course. It was loaded with stink bugs. And the stink bugs at a certain time of the year, they come out of the walls and they crawl all over things. And one of their favorite places was right behind where the teacher's desk sat. And they'd fall out on the desk. I don't know if they ever fell out on me, but they smelled terrible. When a stink bug would come, you'd know a stink bug was there. And Alice and Hester, that was those two eighth graders, they sat there and they had a stink bug. And they had long hair at that time, the girls did, that is. And they'd pull hair out and they'd drive this stink bug. Or they'd put it down and then they'd let it walk across and raise it up. And they'd play and playing with that stink bug. And I got so aggravated at them, you know, because I knew the time was short. I think they only had six weeks left of that after I got there. I think there was only six weeks left of school. And of course, I just had to get them through that eighth grade, I figured. And here they were playing with a stink bug. I one day I got so aggravated, I said, if you two girls, if I even see you looking at a stink bug, I'm going to make you eat it. And I could have bit my tongue afterward because I thought, good grief, I can't make them eat a stink bug. But I watched them like a hawk. And they said they could just feel that stink bug crawling all over them, but they didn't dare look to see.

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: And I said, there seems to have been a little more discipline in schools then than there. Quite so.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: How many students did you have in each class?

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: Well, they varied. I suppose around 20 and 24. There was 24 out to that one in the Ploose school up there. That was a big school too. I think bigger. That was considered quite big because when you get all the grades, then out here at the home school, they pulled in one on me because I think I had all eight grades there too, including the first grade. And then at Christmas, why they started another one. So I really had two first graders. I had a beginner and the first grade it had been through, you know. And one school. I remember that was that one. Which school that was, but I had to do a little algebra work too with the eighth grader. He's gone there.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: You had to be pretty adaptable then, didn't you?

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: Yeah, you really did have to be. And the kids, that was why I say they had to be independent. Because when you take that many kids, that many grades, you don't have very much time to spend with each individual. They had to be. Of course, they could progress kind of at their own level. If there were like three kids in the age, you didn't hold them all in the same place. You kind of worked. Well, they had to more or less because you didn't have time. You had just so much time. You'd bring them up on the bench in front and you'd hold a class. And you'd have that one instruction period that you'd have to teach them how they were supposed to do the work that was ahead. And they had to listen. Of course, they could come to your desk and if they got stalled, but they were sort of discouraged to that to a certain extent. Because I figured if they were listening like they should have been during class, they were supposed to come.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Then they weren't supposed to have any questions, right?

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: Right. Well, they'd have questions and you always had to take time for it. But they were supposed to work pretty independently and they had to listen pretty close. And they didn't have reams of stuff to go through like they did now. When you'd have a little spelling book like this, you just had words to learn to spell and to look up the meanings because you had to know the meaning too. Because orally, you'd have them give them sentences so they'd have to know what they meant. As well as how to spell them. But it wasn't all written out for them to fill in the blanks. They had to go to the dictionary. Dictionaries were well used in those days. I suppose maybe they are now, I don't know. But they, I guess each kid had a dictionary in his desk too. Because they had to look him up and find it out.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: What other subjects did you have besides the, what subjects did you have?

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: Well, we had just plain old reading, writing, arithmetic, geography, I want you to know, science and physiology and language, grammar. Physiology.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: What did you cover?

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: Well, physiology would be the human body. Mm -hmm. And like that, that was part of, and current events which I hated and loathed with all my soul. I didn't pass it the first time either. Had to go back a second time. That reminds me of a thought. I was wondering, you should have been right about the right age to hear some of the current events of Troy at that time. Such as when Marshall Hayes was killed and a few things like that. Well, I should have, you know, but to be perfectly honest, it was, a lot of that I got later because in those, that time, we didn't have a newspaper, I guess. And we didn't come to town. And as far as meeting together, we didn't meet together often enough to do much of that.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: So a lot of that I didn't, but what year was this Hayes killed?

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: I remember that we did hear about it, but it seems to me like that occurred before we came, or after we came. I don't know whether, I don't remember the year. You don't ever remember meeting him either. No. Just his. The stories of him then. I remember going down and they said it was behind that stump was where the guy was. There were stumps all over the place, of course.

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: Because there were trees, and Troy was just hidden in the trees pretty well, I guess. And a lot of this has been cleared since was stump lamb they had there. And, of course, the little old fashioned stores were there. And I should remember the year that they put gravel on the streets, because that was such a wonderful thing. They graveled the street. We didn't have to wade in the mud, slop, slop, and slurp, slurp the horses. But I don't recall exactly.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Were you the one that told me the story about the man in the mud? About what?

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: About the man in the mud in Main Street.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: Mud?

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: Yeah. Well, there was plenty of mud in Main Street. Yeah, that was for sure, but everybody had to.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: About the man walking across the board and he saw a hat down in the mud?

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: No.

speaker: Karen Purtee
words: No, you didn't tell me that story?

speaker: Helena Cartwright Carlson
words: I thought you did. I don't remember that. Okay. But then we had this Ritman Hill that climbed out and that was muddy for a long time. It was a long time before they started putting rocks around on the country roads. And the high water, that was quite an exciting time when they'd have the ridges wouldn't carry the water. They'd wash out. And the meadow would be underwater, you know, needy from the washing. So then that was one of the things we kind of hated to see happen, but they had, eventually that's after we left there though, that they cleared the creek out. And so the water would go on the channel, in a channel, took all of our precious bushes and hideaways that we had.
